title: The for Loop ⚙️
description: The ultimate all-in-one loop for counting.

slides.title: The Compact Loop 🧳
slides.content:
Programmers got tired of forgetting to write the `i++` update inside `while` loops (creating nasty infinite loops).

To fix this, the **`for` loop** was created. It packs all three loop elements (Initialization, Condition, Update) securely into one single line.

slides.title: The for Loop Syntax
slides.content: The top line is structured as: `for (INIT; CONDITION; UPDATE)`.

quizData.question: In the `for` statement `for(int i = 0; i < 10; i++)`, how many times is the Initialization step (`int i = 0`) executed?

options.text: Every time the loop restarts (10 times).
Exactly once, at the very beginning.

quizData.explanation: The first section (the Initialization) fires only once to set up the tracker variable. The other two sections fire every loop.

slides.title: The Infinite For Loop ♾️
slides.content:
Did you know all three parts of a `for` loop are technically optional?

If you leave them all blank like `for(;;)`, you create a loop that runs forever. It is the exact equivalent of `while(1)`.

quizData.question: Which of these loops will count DOWN from 10 to 1?
quizData.explanation: We start at 10, keep going while `i` is greater than 0, and subtract 1 (`i--`) on every loop.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## while vs for

When should you use which? It's simple: If you know EXACTLY how many times to loop (like 1 to 10), use a `for` loop.

If you are waiting for an unpredictable event (like waiting for the user to type 'Quit'), use a `while` loop.
